Jonathan Do, Esq. 408-287-4444 Fusion Law Firm for Plaintiff.
Byron Chin, Esq. 415-273-7597 Kilpatrick Townsend & Stockton LLP for Defendant Natalie Ho who is also present via Zoom.

Parties state appearance. The Court announces the tentative ruling. The Court states a timely notice of contest the TR was received.

ARGUED. After full arguments, the Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

Defendant Natalie Ho's Motion to Compel and Request for Sanctions.

Taken Under Submission.***

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

***After the hearing, the court signs an order. (see scanned order for details)***

Motion To Compel Production Of Documents And Further Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, Set One And Set Two And Request For Sanctions
Off Calendar Apr-24-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Plaintiff Sabrina Mai's motion to compel further responses to requests for production (set one and two) is DENIED.

First, Plaintiff failed to provide a separate statement in compliance with California Rules of Court, rule 3.1345. The court is left without a single document that allows it to determine the full request(s) and the full response(s) at issue, as well as the factual and legal reasons for compelling further responses or production as to each request for which the response is in dispute. (See California Rules of Court, rule 3.1345(c).) A compliant separate statement is essential to the court's adjudication of such a motion; its absence is grounds to deny the motion. The court does not grant leave for a concise outline in lieu of a separate statement; in any event, no concise outline was filed with the moving papers. (See Code of Civil Procedure section 2031.310(b)(3).)

Second, the motion is moot since Defendant Natalie Ho made extensive document productions on March 16 and 30, after Plaintiff filed this motion. (Peterson Decl., par. 9.)

The request for sanctions is denied.

Defendant shall prepare a proposed order which repeats the above text verbatim and email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the time set for hearing.

A proposed order is submitted for the court's review and signature.

Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

MINI MINUTES:
Appearances (by Zoom):
Jonathan Do, Esq. 408-287-4444 Fusion Law for Plaintiff.
Kristine Craig, Esq. 415-273-4331 Kilpatrick Townsend & Stockton LLP for Defendant.

ARGUED. Ms. Craig contests and presents argument. The Court comments. Mr. Do responds. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

1 - PLAINTIFF SABRINA MAI's MOTION TO STRIKE CROSS COMPLAINT.

Sabrina Mai's Motion to Strike is taken under submission.*** The Court will prepare an order.

Visiting Judge: Cindee Mayfield, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/CM)

***The court signs an order after the hearing. (see scanned order for details)***

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To Amend Cross-Complaint; Memorandum Or Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Dec-10-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Jonathan Do, Esq. 408-287-4444 Fusion Law for Plaintiff.
Kristine Craig, Esq. 415-273-4331 Kilpatrick Townsend & Stockton LLP for Defendant.

ARGUED. Ms. Craig contests and presents argument. The Court comments. Mr. Do responds. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

2 - CROSS COMPLAINANT NATALIE HO'S Motion For Leave To Amend Cross-Complaint.

Natalie Ho's Motion for Leave to Amend Cross-Complaint is DENIED.

While Code of Civil Procedure section 473(a)(1) affords the Court broad discretion to allow amendments "in furtherance of justice," and such amendments are ordinarily to be liberally permitted (Hirsa v. Superior Court (1981) 118 Cal.App.3d 486, 488-489), that general rule does not apply in the Anti-SLAPP context. Section 425.16 is designed to provide a swift and efficient mechanism for disposing of claims arising from protected activity at an early stage. Allowing a Ms. Mai to amend after the filing of a special motion to strike would undermine the statute's purpose by enabling a Ms. Mai to avoid the required prima facie showing. As the court explained in Simmons v. Allstate Ins. Co. (2001) 92 Cal.App.4th 1068, 1073, granting leave to amend in response to an Anti-SLAPP motion would be contrary to the statutory scheme and would frustrate legislative intent.

Because amendment is not permitted once an Anti-SLAPP motion is pending, leave to amend is denied.

A proposed order is submitted for the court's signature.

Visiting Judge: Cindee Mayfield, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/CM)
